title: Comprehensive AI Hardware Comparison 2025: Energy Efficiency, Performance, and Environmental Impact Across GPU, TPU, and Custom Accelerators
text:
Performance per watt is the most critical metric for evaluating AI accelerator efficiency, as it directly determines the total cost of ownership and environmental impact of AI infrastructure. The NVIDIA A100 SXM achieves 312 TFLOPS of FP16 performance with sparsity at 400W, yielding 0.78 TFLOPS/W. The NVIDIA H100 SXM delivers 1,979 TFLOPS of FP16 with sparsity at 700W, yielding 2.83 TFLOPS/W. The NVIDIA H200 SXM shares the same compute die as the H100, delivering 1,979 TFLOPS at 700W for 2.83 TFLOPS/W, with improvements coming from memory rather than compute. The NVIDIA B200 reaches approximately 4,500 TFLOPS of FP16 with sparsity at 1,000W, yielding 4.5 TFLOPS/W. The AMD MI300X delivers 1,307 TFLOPS of FP16 with sparsity at 750W, yielding 1.74 TFLOPS/W. The Google TPU v5p achieves 459 TFLOPS of BF16 at approximately 250W, yielding approximately 1.84 TFLOPS/W. The Google TPU v6e is estimated to deliver approximately 918 TFLOPS of BF16 at approximately 200W, yielding approximately 4.59 TFLOPS/W. The Intel Gaudi 3 delivers approximately 1,835 TFLOPS of BF16 at 900W, yielding approximately 2.04 TFLOPS/W. The AWS Inferentia2 achieves 190 TFLOPS at approximately 110W, yielding approximately 1.73 TFLOPS/W, optimized specifically for inference workloads. The AWS Trainium2 is estimated to deliver approximately 840 TFLOPS at approximately 350W, yielding approximately 2.40 TFLOPS/W.
Memory capacity is the critical bottleneck for serving large language models, as model weights must fit in accelerator memory for efficient inference. The A100 provides 80GB of HBM2e, the H100 provides 80GB of HBM3, the H200 provides 141GB of HBM3e, and the B200 provides 192GB of HBM3e. On the AMD side, the MI300X provides 192GB of HBM3 and the MI325X provides 256GB of HBM3e. The Google TPU v5p provides 95GB of HBM2e, while the Intel Gaudi 3 provides 128GB of HBM2e. A Llama 2 70B model in FP16 requires approximately 140GB of memory, so only the H200, B200, MI300X, MI325X, and GB200/GB300 can serve it on a single accelerator without model parallelism. A 405B parameter model requires approximately 810GB in FP16, needing 6 H100 GPUs but only 5 MI300X accelerators or 4 MI325X accelerators.
Memory bandwidth directly impacts tokens-per-second in autoregressive LLM inference, since each generated token must read all model weights from memory. The A100 provides 2.0 TB/s of memory bandwidth, the H100 provides 3.35 TB/s, the H200 provides 4.8 TB/s, and the B200 provides 8.0 TB/s. The AMD MI300X provides 5.3 TB/s and the MI325X provides 6.0 TB/s. The Google TPU v5p provides 2.76 TB/s, and the Intel Gaudi 3 provides 3.7 TB/s. Higher memory bandwidth translates directly to faster token generation for memory-bandwidth-bound inference workloads.
The energy cost of training large AI models is substantial and growing rapidly. Training GPT-3 with 175 billion parameters consumed an estimated 1,287 MWh of electricity. Training LLaMA-3 70B consumed approximately 2,700 MWh on H100 clusters, based on estimates from Meta's published reports. Training GPT-4 is estimated to have consumed approximately 50 GWh, though this figure has not been officially disclosed by OpenAI. A single H100 GPU running at its full 700W TDP for 24 hours a day over one year consumes 6,132 kWh, or 6.13 MWh. A cluster of 1,000 H100 GPUs running for one year would consume approximately 6,132 MWh before accounting for cooling overhead. With a Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE) of 1.3, which is typical for US datacenters, the total facility energy consumption rises to approximately 7,972 MWh. At an electricity cost of $0.05 per kWh, the annual electricity cost for 1,000 GPUs is approximately $400,000. The carbon footprint of such a cluster, using the US average grid carbon intensity of 0.4 kg CO2 per kWh, would be approximately 2,453 metric tons of CO2.
Inference energy consumption is often overlooked but typically dominates the total energy lifecycle of an AI model. A single H100 inference query for a large language model generating approximately 500 tokens consumes between 0.001 and 0.01 kWh, depending on model size and batch configuration. At scale, ChatGPT serving is estimated to consume approximately 564 MWh per day based on estimates of query volume. Inference typically accounts for 60-90% of total AI energy consumption over a model's entire lifecycle. Batch processing is 5 to 10 times more energy efficient per token than real-time serving, because batch processing maximizes GPU utilization. Quantization techniques such as FP8, INT8, and FP4 can reduce inference energy consumption by 30-60% with minimal accuracy loss.
Interconnect bandwidth is essential for multi-GPU training efficiency and determines the maximum number of accelerators that can be used before communication overhead becomes the bottleneck. NVIDIA NVLink 4, used in the H100, provides 900 GB/s of bidirectional bandwidth. NVIDIA NVLink 5, used in the B200, doubles this to 1,800 GB/s. AMD Infinity Fabric in the MI300X provides 896 GB/s. Google's ICI interconnect for the TPU v5p uses a proprietary high-bandwidth mesh topology. The Intel Gaudi 3 integrates 24 200GbE RDMA ports directly on the chip, reducing the need for external network switches. AWS NeuronLink provides 384 GB/s of chip-to-chip bandwidth for Trainium and Inferentia chips.
Cooling and datacenter design have become critical considerations as AI accelerator power consumption increases. Air cooling is used for GPUs up to approximately 700W, including the A100, H100, and most MI300X deployments. Liquid cooling is mandatory for accelerators exceeding 700W, including the B200 at 1,000W and the GB200 and GB300 systems. Liquid cooling reduces the PUE from the typical air-cooled range of 1.3-1.5 down to 1.05-1.1, saving 15-30% on total facility energy consumption. Direct-to-chip liquid cooling is becoming the standard for next-generation AI hardware. Air-cooled datacenters consume 1-2 liters of water per kWh for evaporative cooling, while liquid-cooled systems can reduce water consumption by 70-90%.
Generational improvements in energy efficiency demonstrate significant progress across all major AI hardware vendors. From Ampere to Hopper, the A100 to H100 transition delivered approximately 3.5x better FP16 performance while improving performance per watt by approximately 1.75x. From Hopper to Blackwell, the H100 to B200 transition delivered approximately 2.3x better FP16 performance with approximately 1.6x better performance per watt. Over three generations from A100 to B200, the cumulative improvement is approximately 8x in raw performance and approximately 2.8x in performance per watt. AMD improved from the MI250X to the MI300X with approximately 3.4x FP16 performance while maintaining a similar power envelope. Google improved from TPU v4 to v6e with approximately 3.3x performance and approximately 2.7x better efficiency per dollar.
The environmental impact of AI hardware extends beyond operational energy consumption to include manufacturing, location-dependent carbon intensity, and sustainability strategies. The embodied carbon of manufacturing a single GPU in the A100 or H100 class is estimated at 150-300 kg of CO2. Operational carbon dominates total lifecycle emissions after approximately 6-12 months of heavy use, making energy efficiency the primary factor in long-term environmental impact. Location has an enormous effect on carbon intensity: running AI workloads in Quebec, which relies on hydroelectric power at 0.002 kg CO2 per kWh, produces 450 times less carbon than running in West Virginia, which relies heavily on coal at 0.9 kg CO2 per kWh. Renewable energy purchasing through Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs) can offset operational emissions. Hardware refresh cycles present a trade-off: upgrading from A100 to H100 can reduce energy per FLOP by approximately 45%, but manufacturing new hardware carries its own carbon cost that must be amortized over the lifetime of the replacement hardware.
